lessons.title: RAG Architecture: Chunking, Embedding, and Vector Retrieval
lessons.title_es: Arquitectura RAG: chunking, embedding y recuperación vectorial
lessons.description: Build the technical foundation of a retrieval-augmented generation system that returns accurate, grounded answers.
lessons.description_es: Construye la base técnica de un sistema de generación aumentada por recuperación que devuelve respuestas precisas y fundamentadas.
lessons.content:
What RAG Actually Solves

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) solves the two core limitations of LLMs used alone: hallucination (the model invents facts not in training data) and knowledge cutoff (the model doesn't know anything that happened after its training date). RAG gives the LLM access to a searchable knowledge base at inference time — it retrieves relevant documents and passes them as context before generating the answer. The model can only state what the retrieved documents say, dramatically reducing hallucination on factual queries.

The Four-Stage RAG Pipeline

  1. Ingestion: Load source documents (PDFs, URLs, database records, spreadsheets). Extract clean text — strip headers/footers from PDFs, remove HTML from web pages. This step is underestimated. Bad extraction = bad answers, regardless of the rest of the pipeline.
  2. Chunking: Split documents into retrievable segments. Chunk size is the most important RAG parameter. Too large: retrieval returns irrelevant context. Too small: retrieval misses context needed to answer fully. Start with 512-token chunks with 50-token overlap — the overlap prevents answer gaps at chunk boundaries.
  3. Embedding: Pass each chunk through an embedding model to produce a dense vector representation. Best models: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large (best accuracy, proprietary), Cohere embed-v3 (strong multilingual), nomic-embed-text (open-source, self-hostable). Store vectors in a vector database.
  4. Retrieval + Generation: At query time, embed the user's question, perform approximate nearest-neighbor search in the vector DB, retrieve top-k chunks, inject into LLM context, generate answer with citations.

Chunking Strategies

Fixed-size chunking: Simple, consistent, but ignores document structure. Good default for homogeneous documents.

Semantic chunking: Splits on semantic boundaries (paragraphs, headings, sentences). Better for structured documents like policies and procedures. Use LangChain's SemanticChunker or implement with spaCy sentence boundary detection.

Hierarchical chunking: Creates parent documents (full sections) and child chunks (paragraphs). Retrieval finds the child chunk; context window receives the parent. Best accuracy for long-form technical documents.

Vector Database Selection

Pinecone: Managed, scales to millions of vectors, no infrastructure. Best for production without a DevOps team. Cost: ~$70/mo for starter.

Weaviate or Qdrant: Self-hostable, hybrid search (vector + keyword), better for complex filtering. Good choice when you need to filter by metadata (e.g.,

).

pgvector: Postgres extension. Best if you're already on Postgres and don't need sub-10ms latency. Zero additional infrastructure.

lessons.title: Building Your Knowledge Base: Ingestion Pipelines and Source Management
lessons.title_es: Construcción de tu base de conocimiento: pipelines de ingesta y gestión de fuentes
lessons.description: Design ingestion pipelines that keep your RAG knowledge base current and accurate as sources change.
lessons.description_es: Diseña pipelines de ingesta que mantengan tu base de conocimiento RAG actualizada y precisa a medida que cambian las fuentes.
lessons.content:
Source Inventory First

Before writing a line of ingestion code, conduct a Source Inventory: list every knowledge source in your organization, classify by type (structured vs. unstructured), estimate update frequency, and rate relevance to the agent's query types. Common sources for a business RAG agent: company website, product documentation, pricing sheets, SOPs, case studies, FAQ documents, CRM knowledge articles, and email templates.

Prioritize sources by query coverage — the 20% of sources that answer 80% of questions. Start there and expand. A small, high-quality knowledge base outperforms a large, noisy one.

Document Loaders by Source Type

  • PDFs: Use pdfplumber (best for structured PDFs with tables) or pymupdf (fastest, good for text-heavy PDFs). Avoid pdfminer for complex layouts.
  • Web pages: Firecrawl API for authenticated/JS-rendered pages; BeautifulSoup for simple static HTML. Strip navigation, footers, and cookie banners before embedding.
  • Google Docs/Sheets: Google Workspace API. Export Docs as plain text, Sheets as CSV. Map column headers to semantic descriptions before chunking.
  • Notion databases: Notion API. Export page content + database properties. Treat each Notion page as a document.
  • Slack/email: Use with caution — high noise, low signal. Apply filtering rules (only index threads marked as resolved, only emails in specific labels).

Metadata Tagging Strategy

Every chunk should carry metadata that enables filtered retrieval: source_url, document_type, last_updated, department, topic_tags, confidence_level. At query time, apply metadata filters before vector search. Example: a customer service bot should only retrieve from document_type: [faq, policy, product], not internal strategy memos.

Keeping the Knowledge Base Fresh

A RAG system is only as good as its freshness. Implement two update mechanisms:

  • Incremental sync: Check source documents for changes on a schedule (hourly for critical sources, daily for stable ones). Compare file hashes or last_modified timestamps. Re-embed only changed chunks.
  • Triggered updates: Webhook from your CMS or document system triggers immediate re-ingestion when a document is published or updated. Critical for pricing, policy, and compliance documents.

Mark stale chunks with a stale: true flag and exclude them from retrieval until re-embedded. Never serve answers from chunks older than your defined freshness threshold for time-sensitive content types.

lessons.title: Query Understanding, Hybrid Search, and Answer Quality
lessons.title_es: Comprensión de consultas, búsqueda híbrida y calidad de respuestas
lessons.description: Improve retrieval accuracy with hybrid search techniques and query preprocessing.
lessons.description_es: Mejora la precisión de recuperación con técnicas de búsqueda híbrida y preprocesamiento de consultas.
lessons.content:
Why Pure Vector Search Fails on Short Queries

Vector similarity search excels at semantic understanding (

even with no shared keywords). But it struggles with exact-match queries — specific product names, model numbers, person names, and dates. A hybrid of vector + keyword search solves both.

Hybrid Search Architecture

BM25 keyword search ranks documents by term frequency and inverse document frequency — the same algorithm powering Elasticsearch and Solr. It handles exact matches and rare terms better than vector search.

Combine BM25 and vector search scores with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): merge the ranked lists from both methods, weighting recent high-rank positions more than low-rank ones. RRF consistently outperforms either method alone without requiring parameter tuning per query type. Weaviate and Qdrant have native hybrid search with RRF. For Pinecone, implement BM25 separately using Pinecone's sparse vector support.

Query Preprocessing

Transform the raw user query before embedding it for retrieval. Three preprocessing techniques that materially improve accuracy:

  • Query expansion: Use an LLM to generate 3 alternative phrasings of the user's question, embed all four, retrieve for each, and merge results. Catches vocabulary mismatches between query and documents.
  • Hypothetical Document Embedding (HyDE): Ask the LLM to generate a hypothetical answer to the user's question, then use that hypothetical answer as the embedding query. Counterintuitively, a verbose hypothetical answer is a better embedding target than a short question.
  • Query classification: Route different query types to different retrieval strategies. Factual lookups → strict keyword match. Conceptual questions → pure vector. Procedural questions → hybrid.

Grounding and Citation

Every RAG response should cite its sources. Implement citation by including the source metadata (document title, URL, section) in each retrieved chunk, and prompt the LLM to reference sources explicitly:

This serves two purposes: it increases user trust and it makes hallucination detectable (if the cited source doesn't support the claim, the answer is wrong).

Add an answer confidence score: if the top retrieved chunks have low similarity to the query (<0.75 cosine similarity), add a disclaimer:
Never let the model confidently answer when retrieval quality is low.

lessons.title: Deploying and Monitoring a Production RAG Agent
lessons.title_es: Despliegue y monitoreo de un agente RAG en producción
lessons.description: Ship a RAG agent to production and build the observability layer that catches failures before users do.
lessons.description_es: Lanza un agente RAG a producción y construye la capa de observabilidad que detecta fallos antes que los usuarios.
lessons.content:
Deployment Architecture Options

Three deployment patterns for production RAG agents:

  • API wrapper: REST API (FastAPI or Node/Express) that takes a query, runs retrieval, calls the LLM, returns a structured response. Stateless, horizontally scalable. Best for integrating into existing products.
  • Chat widget: Embed a conversational UI (Voiceflow, Chatbase, or custom) on your website or app. Users ask questions in natural language. Multi-turn conversation state managed server-side.
  • Internal Slack/Teams bot: Deployed as a slash command or mention-triggered bot. Employees query company knowledge without leaving their workflow. Highest adoption rate for internal use cases.

Evaluation Before Launch

Create a RAG evaluation set of 50–100 question-answer pairs covering your most important use cases. For each question, document the expected answer and the expected source document. Before launch, run the full pipeline against this eval set and measure:

  • Retrieval recall: Does the retrieval return the correct source document in the top-3 results? Target: >85%.
  • Answer faithfulness: Does the LLM's answer match the retrieved document content? Evaluate with an LLM judge prompt.
  • Answer relevance: Does the answer address the user's actual question? Another LLM judge metric.

Don't launch until retrieval recall exceeds 80% on your eval set.

Production Monitoring

Log every query and response. The minimum observability data per request: query text, retrieved chunks (with similarity scores), final response, response latency, and user feedback signal (thumbs up/down if you have it). Ship to a structured logging system (Datadog, Langsmith, or even a simple Postgres table).

Monitor for query patterns that produce low retrieval scores — these are gaps in your knowledge base. Weekly: review the 10 lowest-similarity queries from the past 7 days. Create documents that answer these gaps and ingest them. Over 4–8 weeks, this process covers the long tail of questions your users actually ask.

Cost Optimization

Embedding costs are fixed (one-time per document). Inference costs scale with usage. Optimize inference costs with: caching (cache answers to repeated identical queries for 24 hours), model routing (use a smaller/cheaper model for simple factual lookups, reserve the full model for complex reasoning), and context compression (summarize retrieved chunks before passing to the LLM instead of passing raw chunk text). These three tactics typically reduce inference cost by 40–60% without measurable quality loss.
